body: I would try to avoid too much forward-looking and "in this implementation" in the comments -- remember, this is what the API docs are generated from.  What do you want this to say to someone who is trying to understand the API?
I think this should also say what happens if it is not specified

I would move the details of PodGroup into this struct on the PodGroupName field.  Imagine this supported 10 different kinds of scheduling group, you wouldn't put all the docs here on the parent, you would put them on the individual fields.
good question - it looks like we do not generally do that.
optional fields should be pointers.  We can (and do, sometimes) use non-pointer values where the zero-value is out-of-bounds, but as a more general pattern, just use pointers for optionals.

A few things here:

1) add a comment to the effect of "this is a one-of set, but there's only one option present at the moment"

2) Don't treat a pointer to "" as equivalent to nil.  If the field is nil you can can return an error (`Invalid()` is correct here, as it aligns with DV: staging/src/k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/api/validate/union.go.  If they specify `""` then they should get an Invalid error about the name format.

the field name is already in the error (as a field.Path), so the detail string shoudl not repeat the name.  Otherwise you get something like:  "Invalid: path.to.podGroupName: podGroupName must not be empty".  Just "must not be empty" is OK.  In DV it would be tagged as a union, so you would get something like "must specify one of: podGroupName" on the parent, but we can adjust that when we are ready to use DV in pods.

@lalitc375 @aaron-prindle @yongruilin

This is a situation I don't think I have seen before -- we want to EOL a v1alpha1 type but not the whole apigroup.

@liggitt for consult.

Getting rid of a whole alpha apigroup is considered acceptable because it was alpha anyway, so we're OK with any detritus left behind.  But getting rid of a type while keeping the apigroup...I don't know if we have done that before.  There's a kind of assumption that if a "lower" apigroup exists, a type in a "higher" group can be accessed through the lower API, which would fail if it doesn't exist.

But actually, is that true?  If we were to add a new type to "core", it would need to go thru v1alpha1 and we probably would not re-populate pod and service and replicationcontroller and ... in v1a1, right?  This is a corner of the apigroup versioning that has bothered me for a very long time.
identifier for the PodGroup or the PodGroupTemplate ?

Is it allowed for this to not be specified?  What does that mean?  IOW, can you have PodGroups that were created NOT from a template?  And while we are on that, can a pod group template come from somewhere other than a workload?
What does it mean if this is not specified?  Optional fields need to describe their present and not-present semantics.
If it is actually required, it should be marked as required in the API, I added comments there, too
@yongruilin is it possible for this to be DV-native and just not have handwritten validation at all?
I think the fields probably are required, let's discuss in that context - we should not need an exception here

Would you put `DesiredCount` inside the `PodGroup.Spec.SchedulingPolicy.Gang`?  Or would you define a parallel `PodGroup.Spec.SchedulingConfig.Gang` ?

```
PodGroup:
    metadata:
        name: my-pod-group
    spec:
        schedulingPolicy:
            gang:
                minCount: 5
                desiredCount: 10
```

vs.

```
PodGroup:
    metadata:
        name: my-pod-group
    spec:
        schedulingPolicy:
            gang:
                minCount: 5
        schedulingConfig:
            gang:
                desiredCount: 10
```

The former means you either a) put `desiredCount` in and just tell people "don't configure that in the template" (or at least define the behavior when they do) or b) you deep-copy the schema and commit to not colliding.

The latter means you deep-copy the skeleton of the schema (`basic`, `gang`, ...) and impose the UX on users.
I would love to not review any handwritten validation at all...is it possible?  @yongruilin @lalitc375 @aaron-prindle

In DV "immutable" means "NoModify" + "NoSet" + "NoUnset".  Just specifying "NoModify" means that it can change from not-specified to a valid value, and then not be changed again.  I would assume NoModify usually wants NoUnset, too.

Speculative, but I need to ask.  Considering the name of this field:

Can you see a future where we might want OTHER kinds of options that are not a "group"?  E.g. Would "SchedulingPolicy" or "SchedulingConfig" be even more future-safe?  We already have a dozen fields related to scheduling, so maybe this point is not significant.

Alternately, can you see how we might want to frame this as "PodGroup" (but not what this PR defines, more general) or "GroupPolicy" or "GroupConfig" or something?  Right now it says "Scheduling" but it also says "also impacts other lifecycle aspects of a Pod".

So I just want to probe -- is this the best name?  It's more than scheduling and maybe more than just groups?  I will accept "we think it is the best name", but I just ask you to think on it.
Comment along the lines of "Exactly one of these fields must be specified"

small nit: "the lifecycle" - English demands the "the" :)

Same for "the scheduling perspective"

Is this supposed to be allowed to update from unset -> set after creation?  The way it is specc'ed now, it is.  Is that intended @tosi3k ?

If so, perhaps change "When set..." to "Once set...", which implies that it may be set one time.
And consider if this needs `NoUnset` -- Using NoModify without NoUnset means someone could modify it by changing "" -> "abc" -> "" -> "def" -- and you should have a test for this!

Given the discussion around the meaning of `required` WRT openapi vs. k8s, perhaps this should ALSO specify `minItems=1`.  Right now `required` implies that, but openapi would allow `podGroupTemplates=[]` (since that satisfies openapi "required").  We don't actually have a minItems tag yet, but it's trivial to add and when we do it would apply to this.

@lalitc375 what do you think?  Worthwhile or redundant?

There is an age-old debate about "kind" vs "resource".  Kind is ambiguous -- a group CAN have multiple resources with the same Kind.  It's not common, but it's allowed.  Usually when we have a ref to an object of unknown bounds (i.e. the thing which reads this ref does not know a priori what a Kind means), then we switcht to Resource.  It's somewhat less pleasant UX and it's an ugly inconsistency (sometimes kind, sometimes resource), but that's the rule we have laid out.

IOW:

```
    kind: Deployment
```

becomes

```
    resource: deployments
```

Am I right in reading this as an open-ended reference?  I will open a discussion on api-reviews slack, in case sentiment has swung, but last I looked....
The "+oneOf" tag was a placeholder while we developed DV -- `+k8s:unionMember` obviates it, and you can get rid of it.
I think we should still comment that "Exactly one of these fields must be specified".
Same question as Kevin -- I do not want to add fields that say both `+optional` and `+k8s:required`.  That just means we don't know.  I think it would be better to just make it optional

Let's talk about the `alpha` designation here.

What `alpha` means in DV is that the handwritten validation is authoritative, and DV is checked against that to find any inconsistencies.  This being a new API, I don't WANT handwritten code.  I don't want to think about it and I don't want to review it :)

The DV gate is locked on and this is an alpha API group. I think we can say "DV is the only validation for this group" (which is the goal of DV!!).  If we set `WithDeclarativeEnforcement()` in strategy, we can remove most or all of the handwritten validation.

@yongruilin
If you think it is important to distinguish "required" from "must be >= 1" then the field needs to be a pointer, if not, 0 is the only way we can tell

Regarding DV `alpha` -- it looks like DV is already authoritative, so we can streamline wby dropping the alpha prefixes and MarkAlpha stuff.

Right, @yongruilin ?
Hah, it looks like I had this same comment last time I reviewed
Won't `ValidatePodGroupName()` already produce an error if the length is 0?  No need to double-check?
Is there ever going to be a use-case for PodGroupsd that do not include schedulingPolicy?  I know this is in the `scheduling` group, buyt I need to ask :)

ACK

@JoelSpeed will linter eventually understand "this struct is a union" so we don't need exceptions?
Should add tests for changing from unspecified to valid and from valid to unspecified, which probes whether or not it is SUPPOSED to work.

> If you have a more generic term that would capture "scheduling, preemption, eviction, etc." then I'm more than happy to hear that. In my personal mental model "scheduling" includes all of that

I'm OK with that answer.  Something more general like "lifecycle" loses too much detail (and has another meaning).  

> it only defines membership in some group.

I would say this field provides a place to put "configuration" or "policy" related to scheduling, the only form of which we have (for now) is a membership in a group.  

Naming things is hard when you can't see the future. :)

I am OK with group, mostly because we already have other "scheduling policy" fields at this level.  When we do core/v2, we can refactor it :)

no, I mean cases where you have two (top-level) resources which use the same Kind (type / schema).  We don't do it in k/k but some downstream things do (or did).  And yes, OwnerRefs do use Kind, so that's a good argument for why this rule should be considered obsolete.

https://kubernetes.slack.com/team/U0AH4GABW

> I'm not sure what open-ended reference means in this context, however - could you elaborate?

Example:  Gateway API allows refereces to arbitrary types,  but the controller for a given Gateway (as per the class) either knows what the {apigroup, kind} means or else it is an error.  That's NOT open-ended; there is a fixed set of possible types that can be referenced, but the set depends on the controller.

Here it could be ANY type in ANY group, and there's no way to know what it might be.

Don't act on this yet - let's see if we can make an argument to kill this rule.

I thought we decided that the `+k8s:alpha` prefix was not needed, but I still see it here.  I know the transition to DV is a little complicated (sorry) - am I missing a reason why this needs to stay alpha?

I think it can be removed for ALL of these DV tags since there is no equivalent handwritten logic?
same as above

https://kubernetes.slack.com/archives/CJUQN3E4T/p1772645746023809

`Kind` is now considered acceptable.
Why no DV tags for this?  `+k8s:listType=map` etc
Corresponding to the other comments about alpha - I see it is just immutable that is still marked alpha, but I am not sure why.  I think it is not needed
Corresponding to other comments - Why are we keeping this handwritten?
Is it because we still need to manually call the ValidateConditions(), which causes dup errors?  If so, maybe a comment here?

@yongruilin -- I think that resources which are new (alpha), which are not doing new handwritten code, do we still need to do this "it's not stable" dance?  

On one hand, the whole apigroup is alpha, so I don't care that the tag is not stable.  I don't want to read handwritten code.

On the other hand, we can't let the API go to GA while the tag is still alpha.

Is this the best we can do?  It's not terrible since it's 1:1 with a handwritten function, but play it out in the future.

A new API is being built.  It needs a new tag.  We add a new tag, but it is not stable.  Does this mean we have to write handwritten code for the new tag-equivalent?  That doesn't seem to mitigate any risk, which is why we need pre-stable tags in the first place.

How could we avoid needing new handwritten?
If this ends up as the last comment, it could be a followup.
It's only a "problem" in that adding DV to metav1.Condition would generate DV for ~every type, which is only a "problem" because we haven't spent time to fix that :)

@aaron-prindle @yongruilin @lalitc375 

Gut-check time: What if we actually started enabling deeper DV?  When will we feel comfortable doing this?
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/137587
